# Tkinter Window Management

Window lifecycle, borderless mode, dragging, and position persistence.

## Window Lifecycle

Windows are built lazily on first show and can be hidden/reshown without rebuilding:

```python
class MainWindow:
    def __init__(self, root: tk.Tk, controller: "AppController", settings: Settings):
        self._root = root
        self._controller = controller
        self._settings = settings
        self._window: tk.Toplevel | None = None

    def show(self) -> None:
        if self._window and self._window.winfo_exists():
            self._window.deiconify()
            self._window.lift()
            self._window.focus_force()
            return
        self._build()

    def hide(self) -> None:
        if self._window and self._window.winfo_exists():
            self._save_position()
            self._window.withdraw()

    @property
    def is_visible(self) -> bool:
        return bool(
            self._window
            and self._window.winfo_exists()
            and self._window.winfo_viewable()
        )
```

### Safety Checks

Always check `winfo_exists()` before operating on a window. Destroyed windows raise `TclError`:

```python
def get_position(self) -> tuple[int | None, int | None]:
    if not self._window or not self._window.winfo_exists():
        return None, None
    try:
        return self._window.winfo_x(), self._window.winfo_y()
    except tk.TclError:
        return None, None
```

## Borderless Windows

Use `overrideredirect(True)` for custom chrome. Combine with `-topmost` for always-on-top overlay behavior.

### Cross-Platform Differences

`overrideredirect` behaves differently on Windows and Linux:

| Behavior | Windows | Linux/X11 |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| Focus | Normal | May not receive focus |
| Taskbar entry | Removed | Varies by window manager |
| Dialog stacking | Dialogs appear in front | Dialogs may appear behind the window |

On Linux, `overrideredirect` opts the window entirely out of the window manager. Expect
focus and stacking issues that do not occur on Windows. `-topmost` is reliable on
Windows but some Linux window managers ignore it.

```python
def _build(self) -> None:
    win = tk.Toplevel(self._root)
    win.overrideredirect(True)
    win.wm_attributes("-topmost", True)

    # Optional: colored border frame for visual boundary
    border_frame = tk.Frame(win, bg=WINDOW_BORDER_COLOR)
    border_frame.pack(fill=tk.BOTH, expand=True)

    content_frame = tk.Frame(border_frame, bg=self._bg, padx=4, pady=4)
    content_frame.pack(fill=tk.BOTH, expand=True, padx=2, pady=2)
```

### Border Frame Pattern

Since `overrideredirect` removes the OS window border, use a colored outer `Frame` with padding to create a visible border:

```
┌─ border_frame (colored bg, 2px padding) ─┐
│ ┌─ content_frame (dark bg, padx/pady) ──┐ │
│ │                                        │ │
│ │  actual widgets here                   │ │
│ │                                        │ │
│ └────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Draggable Windows

Borderless windows need manual drag support. Track the offset on press, apply on motion:

```python
_DRAG_THRESHOLD = 5  # pixels — distinguish click from drag

def _on_drag_start(self, event: Any) -> None:
    self._drag_start_x = event.x
    self._drag_start_y = event.y
    self._dragged = False

def _on_drag_motion(self, event: Any) -> None:
    dx = abs(event.x - self._drag_start_x)
    dy = abs(event.y - self._drag_start_y)
    if dx > _DRAG_THRESHOLD or dy > _DRAG_THRESHOLD:
        self._dragged = True
    if self._dragged:
        x = self._window.winfo_x() + event.x - self._drag_start_x
        y = self._window.winfo_y() + event.y - self._drag_start_y
        self._window.geometry(f"+{x}+{y}")
```

### Drag Threshold

Use a pixel threshold (typically 5px) to distinguish intentional drags from clicks. Without this, single clicks on the window register as tiny drags and suppress click handlers.

### Binding Drag to Multiple Widgets

Bind drag events to every widget in the draggable area. Child widgets consume events by default:

```python
for widget in [border_frame, content_frame, label1, label2]:
    widget.bind("<Button-1>", self._on_drag_start)
    widget.bind("<B1-Motion>", self._on_drag_motion)
```

## Position Persistence

Save window position on hide; restore on show. The `save_settings()` call and
`Settings` dataclass follow the [Settings Persistence](../settings-persistence.md)
standard.

```python
# Save
def _save_position(self) -> None:
    x, y = self.get_position()
    if x is not None:
        self._settings.window_x = x
        self._settings.window_y = y
        save_settings(self._settings)

# Restore
def _build(self) -> None:
    win = tk.Toplevel(self._root)
    # ... configure window ...
    if self._settings.window_x is not None and self._settings.window_y is not None:
        win.geometry(f"+{self._settings.window_x}+{self._settings.window_y}")
```

### When to Save

Save on **hide** or **close**, not on every drag motion. Saving on every pixel of movement causes excessive I/O.

### Wayland Limitation

Window position setting via `geometry()` is broken under XWayland — it affects size
but not position. Tkinter has no native Wayland support (runs via XWayland only).
Position persistence will not work correctly on Wayland-default desktops (Fedora 41+,
Ubuntu 24.04+). There is no Tk-level fix until native Wayland support is implemented.

## Visibility Methods

| Method | Effect |
|--------|--------|
| `withdraw()` | Hide window (still exists in memory) |
| `deiconify()` | Show hidden window |
| `lift()` | Raise above other windows |
| `focus_force()` | Steal focus (use sparingly) |
| `destroy()` | Remove permanently |
| `grid_remove()` | Hide widget but preserve grid config |

## Window Close Protocol

Handle the window manager close button (`X`) explicitly:

```python
win.protocol("WM_DELETE_WINDOW", self._on_close)

def _on_close(self) -> None:
    self._save_position()
    self._window.destroy()
    self._window = None
```

## What to Avoid

- Saving position on every drag motion event — excessive disk I/O on every pixel of movement
- Forgetting `winfo_exists()` checks after window destruction — raises `TclError` on any subsequent widget call
- Using `destroy()` when `withdraw()` is sufficient — forces a full widget rebuild on next show
- Calling `focus_force()` without user-initiated context — Linux WMs may block it as focus stealing; on Windows it succeeds if the app has foreground rights, otherwise Windows blocks it and flashes the taskbar
- Using `overrideredirect` without providing drag support — the user has no way to move the window since the OS title bar is gone
